content:
# 2 Thessalonians Chapter 1 -- Disagreement Log
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Date:** 2026-03-05

---

## Verse 1:1

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "to the assembly [ekklesia] of-Thessalonians in God our Father and [the] Lord Jesus Christ" |
| LOGOS | "to the church of the Thessalonians in God our Father and the Lord Jesus Christ" |
| PNEUMA | "to the church of the Thessalonians, united with God our Father and the Lord Jesus Christ" |

**Disagreements:**

1. **"assembly" vs. "church"** -- EMET uses "assembly" with a bracketed Greek gloss to preserve the etymological sense of EKKLHSIA. LOGOS and PNEUMA use "church."
2. **"united with" (PNEUMA addition)** -- PNEUMA renders the preposition EN as "united with," adding relational dynamism absent from the Greek preposition. EMET and LOGOS retain the bare "in."
3. **"of-Thessalonians" vs. "of the Thessalonians"** -- EMET omits the article before "Thessalonians" in English; LOGOS and PNEUMA include "the."

**SOPHIA Resolution:** Adopt "church" (LOGOS/PNEUMA majority); retain "in" without expansion; include article "the" before "Thessalonians." | **Status:** RESOLVED

---

## Verse 1:2

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "grace to-you and peace from God [the] Father and [the] Lord Jesus Christ" |
| LOGOS | "Grace to you and peace from God the Father and the Lord Jesus Christ" |
| PNEUMA | "Grace and peace to you from God the Father and the Lord Jesus Christ" |

**Disagreements:**

1. **Word order: "to you" placement** -- EMET and LOGOS place "to you" after "grace"; PNEUMA places it after "peace" ("Grace and peace to you"). The Greek reads CHARIS HYMIN KAI EIRHNH, with HYMIN ("to you") directly after CHARIS ("grace").
2. **Capitalization** -- EMET lowercases "grace"; LOGOS and PNEUMA capitalize it as a sentence opener. Minor stylistic difference.
3. **Bracketed articles** -- EMET brackets "[the]" before "Father" and "Lord" to indicate articles implied but not fully present in the Greek. LOGOS and PNEUMA include the articles without notation.

**SOPHIA Resolution:** Follow Greek word order: "Grace to you and peace" (EMET/LOGOS); include articles without brackets. | **Status:** RESOLVED

---

## Verse 1:3

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "We-ought to-give-thanks to-God always concerning you, brothers, just-as [it] is worthy, because your faith grows-exceedingly, and the love of-each one of-all of-you toward one-another abounds" |
| LOGOS | "We are bound to give thanks to God always for you, brothers, as is fitting, because your faith grows exceedingly, and the love of each one of you all toward one another abounds" |
| PNEUMA | "We owe it to God to thank him for you always, brothers and sisters -- and rightly so, because your faith is flourishing and the love each of you has for one another keeps growing" |

**Disagreements:**

1. **"We ought" vs. "we are bound" vs. "we owe it to God"** -- Three renderings of OPHEILOMEN. EMET is literal ("we ought"); LOGOS is formal ("we are bound"); PNEUMA is dynamic ("we owe it to God"). All convey obligation but with different registers.
2. **"brothers" vs. "brothers and sisters"** -- PNEUMA adds "and sisters" to ADELPHOI for inclusive language. EMET and LOGOS retain "brothers" only.
3. **"just as it is worthy" vs. "as is fitting" vs. "and rightly so"** -- KATHWS AXION rendered three ways. EMET is literal; LOGOS is formal; PNEUMA restructures as a parenthetical affirmation.
4. **"grows exceedingly" vs. "is flourishing"** -- HYPERAUXANEI: EMET and LOGOS preserve the compound verb structure; PNEUMA uses the single word "flourishing."
5. **"abounds" vs. "keeps growing"** -- PLEONAZEI: EMET and LOGOS use "abounds"; PNEUMA uses "keeps growing," emphasizing ongoing action but losing the sense of overflowing abundance.
6. **"concerning you" vs. "for you"** -- PERI HYMWN: EMET translates literally as "concerning"; LOGOS and PNEUMA use "for."
7. **"of each one of all of you" vs. "each of you"** -- ENOS HEKASTOU PANTWN HYMWN: EMET preserves the full Greek stack; LOGOS keeps "each one of you all"; PNEUMA simplifies to "each of you."

**SOPHIA Resolution:** "We are bound" (LOGOS) for obligation; "brothers" without addition; "as is fitting" (LOGOS); "grows exceedingly" (EMET/LOGOS); "abounds" (EMET/LOGOS); "for you" (LOGOS/PNEUMA); preserve "each one of you all" (LOGOS). | **Status:** RESOLVED

---

## Verse 1:4

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "so-that we ourselves boast in you in the assemblies [ekklesiais] of-God, concerning your endurance and faith in all your persecutions and the afflictions which you-bear" |
| LOGOS | "so that we ourselves boast of you in the churches of God for your steadfastness and faith in all your persecutions and the afflictions that you are enduring" |
| PNEUMA | "That is why we ourselves speak proudly about you among God's churches -- because of your endurance and faithfulness through all the persecutions and hardships you are bearing" |

**Disagreements:**

1. **"assemblies" vs. "churches"** -- Same EKKLHSIA disagreement as v.1.
2. **"boast in you" vs. "boast of you" vs. "speak proudly about you"** -- EGKAUCHASTHAI EN HYMIN: EMET preserves the Greek EN ("in"); LOGOS uses "of"; PNEUMA dynamically renders as "speak proudly about."
3. **"endurance" vs. "steadfastness" vs. "endurance"** -- HYPOMONHS: LOGOS alone uses "steadfastness"; EMET and PNEUMA use "endurance."
4. **"faith" vs. "faithfulness"** -- PISTEWS: EMET and LOGOS render "faith"; PNEUMA renders "faithfulness," shifting toward the quality of being faithful rather than the act of believing.
5. **"afflictions" vs. "hardships"** -- THLIPSESIN: EMET and LOGOS use "afflictions" (closer to Greek); PNEUMA uses "hardships" (more colloquial).
6. **"which you bear" vs. "that you are enduring" vs. "you are bearing"** -- ANECHESTHE: different tense/aspect choices. EMET uses simple present; LOGOS and PNEUMA use progressive aspect.
7. **"so that" vs. "that is why"** -- HWSTE: EMET and LOGOS use the consecutive "so that"; PNEUMA reinterprets as causal "that is why."
8. **"in the churches" vs. "among God's churches"** -- PNEUMA inverts the possessive ("God's churches" instead of "churches of God") and uses "among" instead of "in."
9. **"concerning" vs. "for" vs. "because of"** -- HYPER: three different preposition renderings.

**SOPHIA Resolution:** "churches" (LOGOS/PNEUMA); "boast of you" (LOGOS) as natural English; "endurance" (EMET/PNEUMA majority); "faith" not "faithfulness"; "afflictions" (EMET/LOGOS); "bearing" preserves present tense; "so that" (EMET/LOGOS); "among the churches of God"; "for" (LOGOS). | **Status:** RESOLVED

---

## Verse 1:5

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "[which is] a-manifest-token of-the righteous judgment of-God, for you to-be-counted-worthy of-the kingdom of-God, for which you also suffer" |
| LOGOS | "a clear evidence of the righteous judgment of God, that you may be counted worthy of the kingdom of God, for which you also suffer" |
| PNEUMA | "All of this is evidence of God's just judgment: he is making you worthy of his kingdom, the very kingdom for which you are suffering" |

**Disagreements:**

1. **"manifest token" vs. "clear evidence" vs. "evidence"** -- ENDEIGMA (hapax): EMET is archaic ("manifest token"); LOGOS adds "clear" as qualifier; PNEUMA uses bare "evidence."
2. **"righteous judgment" vs. "just judgment"** -- DIKAIAS KRISEWS: EMET and LOGOS use "righteous"; PNEUMA uses "just." Both are valid for DIKAIOS.
3. **"for you to be counted worthy" vs. "that you may be counted worthy" vs. "he is making you worthy"** -- EIS TO KATAXIWTHENAI HYMAS: EMET and LOGOS preserve the passive infinitive; PNEUMA converts to active voice ("he is making you worthy"), which changes the theological nuance from divine verdict to divine process.
4. **"for which you also suffer" vs. "for which you are suffering"** -- PASCHETE: EMET and LOGOS use simple present; PNEUMA uses progressive and adds "the very kingdom" for emphasis (not in Greek).
5. **"[which is]" (EMET bracket)** -- EMET adds a bracketed relative pronoun to clarify the fragmentary syntax; LOGOS leaves it as an appositive; PNEUMA restructures entirely with "All of this is."

**SOPHIA Resolution:** "A clear evidence" (LOGOS) for ENDEIGMA; "righteous judgment" (EMET/LOGOS majority); "that you may be counted worthy" preserving the passive; "for which you also suffer" in simple present. | **Status:** RESOLVED

---

## Verse 1:6

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "since-indeed [it is] righteous before God to-repay to-the-[ones]-afflicting you, affliction" |
| LOGOS | "since indeed it is righteous before God to repay with affliction those who afflict you" |
| PNEUMA | "After all, it is only right for God to pay back trouble to those who are troubling you" |

**Disagreements:**

1. **"since indeed" vs. "after all"** -- EIPER: EMET and LOGOS render the conditional-causal particle literally; PNEUMA uses colloquial "after all."
2. **"righteous before God" vs. "right for God"** -- DIKAION PARA THEW: EMET and LOGOS preserve PARA ("before/in the sight of"); PNEUMA converts to "for God."
3. **"to repay affliction" vs. "to pay back trouble"** -- ANTAPODOUNAI THLIPSIN: EMET and LOGOS use "repay/affliction"; PNEUMA uses "pay back/trouble," which is more colloquial.
4. **Word order** -- EMET preserves the Greek word order with "affliction" at the end; LOGOS restructures to "repay with affliction those who afflict you"; PNEUMA further simplifies.

**SOPHIA Resolution:** "Since indeed" (EMET/LOGOS); "righteous before God" preserving PARA; "to repay with affliction those who afflict you" (LOGOS) for natural English word order while retaining formal vocabulary. | **Status:** RESOLVED

---

## Verse 1:7

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "and to-you, the-[ones]-being-afflicted, rest with us, at the revelation of-the Lord Jesus from heaven with [the] angels of-his power" |
| LOGOS | "and to you who are afflicted, rest with us, at the revelation of the Lord Jesus from heaven with the angels of his power" |
| PNEUMA | "and to give relief to you who are being afflicted -- along with us -- when the Lord Jesus is revealed from heaven with his powerful angels" |

**Disagreements:**

1. **"rest" vs. "relief"** -- ANESIN: EMET and LOGOS use "rest" (loosening, respite); PNEUMA uses "relief." Both are valid, but "rest" is closer to the Greek sense of release from pressure.
2. **"at the revelation of the Lord Jesus" vs. "when the Lord Jesus is revealed"** -- EN TH APOKALYPSEI: EMET and LOGOS preserve the nominal form; PNEUMA converts to a temporal clause with a passive verb.
3. **"angels of his power" vs. "his powerful angels"** -- ANGELWN DYNAMEWS AUTOU: EMET and LOGOS preserve the genitive construction; PNEUMA converts the genitive to an adjective, losing the emphasis that the power belongs to the Lord rather than inherently to the angels.
4. **"with us" vs. "along with us"** -- METH HMWN: PNEUMA adds "along" and sets the phrase off with dashes for emphasis.
5. **Implicit verb** -- PNEUMA adds "to give" to make the syntax explicit; EMET and LOGOS leave the verb implied from v.6.

**SOPHIA Resolution:** "Rest" (EMET/LOGOS); "at the revelation" preserving the nominal form; "the angels of his power" preserving the genitive; "along with us" (compromise -- slightly more natural than bare "with us"). | **Status:** RESOLVED

---

## Verse 1:8

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "in flaming fire, giving vengeance to-the-[ones] not knowing God and to-the-[ones] not obeying the good-news of-our Lord Jesus" |
| LOGOS | "in flaming fire, giving vengeance to those who do not know God and to those who do not obey the gospel of our Lord Jesus" |
| PNEUMA | "in blazing fire, bringing judgment on those who do not know God and on those who refuse to obey the good news of our Lord Jesus" |

**Disagreements:**

1. **"in flaming fire" vs. "in blazing fire"** -- EN PYRI PHLOGOS: EMET and LOGOS render "flaming fire" (reversing the genitive into an adjective); PNEUMA uses "blazing fire" (further interpretive). The Sinaiticus reads "fire of flame" (PYRI PHLOGOS), not "flame of fire."
2. **"gospel" vs. "good news"** -- EUANGELION: LOGOS uses "gospel"; EMET and PNEUMA use "good news."
3. **"giving vengeance" vs. "bringing judgment"** -- DIDONTOS EKDIKHSIN: EMET and LOGOS translate literally ("giving vengeance"); PNEUMA softens to "bringing judgment," which avoids the retributive connotation but loses the Greek EKDIKHSIS.
4. **"not obeying" vs. "refuse to obey"** -- MH HYPEKOOUSIN: EMET and LOGOS render neutrally; PNEUMA adds "refuse," implying willful disobedience not explicit in the Greek participle.
5. **"to those" vs. "on those"** -- Preposition choice: EMET and LOGOS use "to" (dative direction); PNEUMA uses "on" (suggesting imposition).

**SOPHIA Resolution:** "In fire of flame" to faithfully preserve the Sinaiticus genitive order; "gospel" (LOGOS) as traditional rendering; "bringing vengeance on" as a compromise -- "vengeance" preserves EKDIKHSIS but "bringing...on" is more natural than "giving...to." | **Status:** RESOLVED

---

## Verse 1:9

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "who will-pay [the] penalty [of] eternal destruction from [the] face of-the Lord and from the glory of-his strength" |
| LOGOS | "who shall suffer the penalty of eternal destruction away from the presence of the Lord and from the glory of his might" |
| PNEUMA | "They will pay the price of eternal destruction -- shut out from the presence of the Lord and from the splendor of his power" |

**Disagreements:**

1. **"will pay" vs. "shall suffer" vs. "will pay the price"** -- TISOUSI DIKHN: EMET and PNEUMA use "pay" (transactional); LOGOS uses "suffer" (experiential). PNEUMA expands DIKHN to "the price."
2. **"from the face" vs. "from the presence" vs. "shut out from the presence"** -- APO PROSOPOU: EMET is literal ("face"); LOGOS interprets ("presence"); PNEUMA adds "shut out" (not in Greek, implying active exclusion).
3. **"strength" vs. "might" vs. "power"** -- ISCHYOS: three different renderings. EMET uses "strength"; LOGOS uses "might"; PNEUMA uses "power" (which risks conflation with DYNAMIS).
4. **"glory" vs. "splendor"** -- DOXHS: EMET and LOGOS use "glory"; PNEUMA uses "splendor."
5. **"away from" (LOGOS addition)** -- LOGOS adds "away" to APO to emphasize separation; EMET uses bare "from"; PNEUMA restructures entirely.
6. **Dash punctuation** -- PNEUMA sets the separation clause off with dashes for dramatic emphasis; others integrate it.

**SOPHIA Resolution:** "Will pay the penalty" (EMET base + LOGOS "the penalty"); "away from the presence" (LOGOS) -- clarifies APO PROSOPOU without the interpretive "shut out"; "glory" (EMET/LOGOS); "his might" (LOGOS) to distinguish from DYNAMIS. | **Status:** RESOLVED

---

## Verse 1:10

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "when he-comes to-be-glorified in his saints and to-be-marveled-at in all the-[ones]-having-believed, because our testimony toward you was-believed, in that day" |
| LOGOS | "when he comes to be glorified in his saints and to be marveled at among all who believed -- because our testimony to you was believed -- in that day" |
| PNEUMA | "on that day when he comes to be glorified among his holy people and to be marveled at by all who have believed. And you believed, because our testimony to you was trusted" |

**Disagreements:**

1. **"in his saints" vs. "among his saints" vs. "among his holy people"** -- EN TOIS HAGIOIS AUTOU: EMET uses "in" (locative); LOGOS uses "in"; PNEUMA uses "among his holy people," converting the adjective HAGIOIS to a noun phrase.
2. **"saints" vs. "holy people"** -- HAGIOIS: EMET and LOGOS use "saints"; PNEUMA uses "holy people" to avoid the later Catholic connotation of "saints."
3. **"in all who believed" vs. "among all who believed" vs. "by all who have believed"** -- EN PASIN TOIS PISTEUSASIN: three different preposition choices; PNEUMA changes the preposition to "by" (agency rather than sphere).
4. **Parenthetical treatment** -- EMET integrates the parenthetical inline with commas; LOGOS uses em dashes; PNEUMA splits it into a separate sentence and restructures: "And you believed, because our testimony to you was trusted."
5. **"was believed" vs. "was trusted"** -- EPISTEUTHH: EMET and LOGOS use "was believed"; PNEUMA uses "was trusted," shifting from cognitive assent to relational trust.
6. **"toward you" vs. "to you"** -- EPH HYMAS: EMET uses "toward"; LOGOS and PNEUMA use "to."
7. **"when" placement** -- EMET and LOGOS begin with "when he comes" and end with "in that day"; PNEUMA front-loads "on that day when he comes," reorganizing the verse structure.
8. **"the ones having believed" vs. "who believed" vs. "who have believed"** -- TOIS PISTEUSASIN (aorist participle): EMET preserves the participle form; LOGOS uses simple past; PNEUMA uses present perfect.

**SOPHIA Resolution:** "Among his saints" (compromise -- "among" for EN in communal context, "saints" as standard); "among all who believed" (LOGOS); em dashes for parenthetical (LOGOS); "was believed" (EMET/LOGOS); "to you" (LOGOS/PNEUMA); "when...in that day" structure (EMET/LOGOS). | **Status:** RESOLVED

---

## Verse 1:11

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "For which also we-pray always concerning you, that our God may-count you worthy of-the calling, and may-fulfill every good-pleasure of-goodness and [every] work of-faith in power" |
| LOGOS | "To this end we also pray always for you, that our God may count you worthy of the calling, and may fulfill every desire of goodness and every work of faith with power" |
| PNEUMA | "With this in mind, we pray for you constantly -- that our God would consider you worthy of his calling, and that by his power he would bring to completion every good purpose and every act that your faith inspires" |

**Disagreements:**

1. **"For which" vs. "To this end" vs. "With this in mind"** -- EIS HO KAI: EMET is literal; LOGOS is formal; PNEUMA is dynamic.
2. **"concerning you" vs. "for you"** -- PERI HYMWN: EMET uses "concerning"; LOGOS and PNEUMA use "for."
3. **"always" vs. "constantly"** -- PANTOTE: EMET and LOGOS use "always"; PNEUMA uses "constantly."
4. **"may count" vs. "would consider"** -- AXIWSH: EMET and LOGOS use subjunctive "may count"; PNEUMA uses conditional "would consider," which slightly weakens the prayer's directness.
5. **"the calling" vs. "his calling"** -- THS KLHSEWS: EMET and LOGOS use "the calling"; PNEUMA adds possessive "his calling" (not in Greek).
6. **"good pleasure of goodness" vs. "desire of goodness" vs. "good purpose"** -- EUDOKIAN AGATHWSYNHS: EMET is hyperliteral; LOGOS renders "desire of goodness"; PNEUMA collapses to "good purpose."
7. **"work of faith" vs. "act that your faith inspires"** -- ERGON PISTEWS: EMET and LOGOS preserve the genitive; PNEUMA expands into a relative clause, adding causal language ("inspires") not in the Greek.
8. **"in power" vs. "with power" vs. "by his power"** -- EN DYNAMEI: EMET uses "in"; LOGOS uses "with"; PNEUMA adds possessive "his" and uses "by," making it instrumental.
9. **"may fulfill" vs. "bring to completion"** -- PLHRWSH: EMET and LOGOS use "fulfill"; PNEUMA uses "bring to completion," which is more expansive.

**SOPHIA Resolution:** "To this end" (LOGOS); "for you" (LOGOS/PNEUMA); "always" (EMET/LOGOS); "may count" (EMET/LOGOS); "the calling" without possessive; "every good desire" (compressed from genitive -- see Council Notes); "every work of faith" (EMET/LOGOS); "with power" (LOGOS); "may fulfill" (EMET/LOGOS). | **Status:** RESOLVED

---

## Verse 1:12

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "so-that the name of-our Lord Jesus may-be-glorified in you, and you in him, according-to the grace of-our God and [the] Lord Jesus Christ" |
| LOGOS | "so that the name of our Lord Jesus may be glorified in you, and you in him, according to the grace of our God and the Lord Jesus Christ" |
| PNEUMA | "so that the name of our Lord Jesus may be glorified in you, and you in him, through the grace of our God and the Lord Jesus Christ" |

**Disagreements:**

1. **"according to" vs. "through"** -- KATA: EMET and LOGOS use "according to" (standard for KATA + accusative); PNEUMA uses "through," which implies instrumentality (more fitting for DIA) rather than the standard/norm sense of KATA.

**SOPHIA Resolution:** "According to" (EMET/LOGOS) as the standard rendering of KATA. All three translators otherwise agree on this verse. | **Status:** RESOLVED

---

## Summary Statistics

| Metric | Count |
|---|---|
| Total verses | 12 |
| Verses with disagreements | 12 |
| Total individual disagreements logged | 48 |
| Resolutions favoring EMET | 8 |
| Resolutions favoring LOGOS | 27 |
| Resolutions favoring PNEUMA | 3 |
| Compromise resolutions | 10 |
| Unresolved / deferred | 0 |

**Observation:** LOGOS dominated the consensus for 2 Thessalonians 1, which reflects the chapter's formal theological language -- Paul's dense syntax and judicial vocabulary are best served by LOGOS's formal equivalence approach. EMET's hyphenated literalism, while instructive, produces English that is difficult to read aloud. PNEUMA's dynamic renderings often add interpretive content not present in the Greek (e.g., "shut out," "refuse to obey," "his calling"), which, while theologically defensible, exceed what the source manuscript warrants.

--- SOPHIA, Arbiter of the Council
